data.title: Amazon's Stock Is Dirt Cheap
data.description:
Despite only a subtle Amazon (AMZN) share price increase since my previous bullish call, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating. Besides its undisputed leadership in e-commerce and cloud, the company also demonstrates strong growth in digital advertising. This thriving business is already quite large, even considering Amazon's scale, and it has the potential to maintain robust growth for longer. Furthermore, Amazon is also expanding into new areas with high potential, such as cloud gaming and autonomous taxis. Besides all these ambitious endeavors around unlocking new revenue growth drivers, I also expect the management to continue cost optimization in 2026, as there is still room to develop. With all these positives, I am sure that the company will demonstrate rapid multiples contraction over the next few years.

Reasons to keep buying AMZN

Amazon is attractively valued if we look at its forward P/E ratio trajectory projected by Wall Street analysts. The estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio is notably below 20, which looks extremely attractive, in my opinion. Besides business-related reasons why I believe Amazon is highly likely to achieve dramatic P/E ratio contraction, there are other factors, such as a strong earnings surprise record and positive earnings revisions.

I have already discussed Amazon's intact dominance in e-commerce and cloud industries in my previous article. Nothing has changed since late September, as both businesses delivered strong double-digit revenue growth in Q3. This dominance alone justifies a bullish case for Amazon, but I want to share other promising growth catalysts in my today's coverage update.

Firstly, I want to highlight the strength of Amazon's digital advertising business. In Q3 2025, it was the fastest-growing business of Amazon for the third quarter in a row. Another big positive sign is that the advertising business' revenue growth is accelerating. Last but not least, Amazon's digital advertising revenue is growing faster than undisputed industry leaders, Google and Meta. Of course, their scale is much larger than that of Amazon's advertising business. However, the fact that Amazon is growing faster suggests that it is able to gain market share at Google's and Meta's expense. Being able to compete with these giants underscores the potential of Amazon's digital advertising business. Since the digital advertising industry is secularly growing, this business line has the potential to continue aggressive growth over the long term.

Besides standalone revenue growth, there is an apparent synergistic effect, as there is e-commerce business and strong AI exposure via AWS. I believe that all three of these assets complement each other, as AI capabilities help to improve digital advertising efficiency, which in turn is likely to convert into higher conversion rates for merchants across the e-commerce platform. High advertising ROI for merchants is highly likely to create a positive experience for them, which will contribute to advertising customer retention. It is also vital to understand that digital advertising has the potential to become a highly profitable business thanks to Amazon's massive scale and extensive available infrastructure from its core businesses.

Apart from this already established but still rapidly growing digital advertising business, Amazon is also expanding into younger areas with even more aggressive growth potential. The company recently launched its Luna cloud gaming service in partnership with Comcast (CMCSA), which is a new business for Amazon. This new business opens new growth opportunities, especially taking into account that Amazon's Subscription Services business is quite successful, as it continued demonstrating double-digit revenue growth in Q3. With the cloud gaming offering, the company now has more options to bundle its Prime offerings, which will likely help to expand its reach. The cloud gaming industry is a high-growth area with a projected 44% CAGR by 2030. It is also vital to mention here that there will likely be synergy with AWS, as its extensive infrastructure is vital for cloud gaming. Moreover, Amazon's presence in digital gaming might also help attract more B2B clients for AWS, offering its infrastructure for various other game publishers. Therefore, this new endeavor looks very promising as well.

Another new business with the potential to demonstrate exponential growth over the long haul is Zoox, the robotaxi business. Amazon is not just an investor or strategic partner but is the sole owner of this business. This business is moving quickly into a commercialization phase, with early users already experiencing Zoox in Las Vegas. The company has already expanded its test footprint to several large U.S. cities like Seattle, Austin, and Miami. The robotaxi industry is considered among the most impactful technological disruptions for the next decade, which means that it will be growing aggressively as well. With Amazon as its parent company, Zoox can rely on massive financial and technological support, which are formidable assets for successfully competing against Tesla and Waymo. Moreover, I see synergistic potential here as well, as Zoox can be used for last-mile delivery, Prime subscription bundling, or Alexa integration.

To understand Amazon's fair value per share, we need the FY2026 EPS estimate from Wall Street and multiply it by the P/E ratio assumption. The EPS forecast for FY2026 is $7.85, and I consider it very reliable because it is an average opinion of 60 Wall Street analysts, which is certainly representative.

The P/E multiple assumption needs to be conservative because Amazon's EPS growth towards $7.85 is not guaranteed. Therefore, I take the midpoint between the current multiple of around 33 and the long-term average of 57, which is 45. Multiplying $7.85 by 45 yields the fair value of $353. It implies a strong 52% upside from current levels, which means that AMZN is dirt cheap.

I think that delivering a robust rally in 2026 is realistic for AMZN not only because of the catalysts related to long-term revenue growth potential but also due to the cost side of the profitability equation. If we look at the company's profitability performance in Q3 2025, there was solid margin expansion. At first glance, the company's operating margin expanded by just 20-bps YoY, from 10.96% to 11.06%. But it is vital to highlight the fact that the operating margin expansion could have been much more aggressive if the company didn't boost its R&D-to-revenue ratio from 11.7% to 13.7%. Under GAAP, R&D is not capitalized, but history proves that in Amazon's case R&D costs are investments and rarely expenses. It is evident from the company's 21% revenue CAGR and 35% EBITDA CAGR over the past decade. The fact that Amazon's EBITDA is growing faster than revenue suggests strong operating leverage, which is a strong bullish factor. Therefore, I am confident that Amazon's $101 billion TTM R&D expenses are likely to pay off.

With the operating margin expanding despite aggressive ramp-up in R&D spending, we have to give credit to the management's commitment to keep costs under control. With strong operating leverage, we are highly likely to see strong EPS growth alongside the projected 11% revenue growth in FY2026. There is another large but frequently overlooked EPS growth catalyst for Amazon. The company is one of the world's largest employers, with approximately 1.5 million employees. In the era of generative AI and robotics, there is vast room to cut payroll costs. Amazon has been cutting thousands of jobs during 2025, and I expect this trend to continue in 2026. Some sources cite extremely aggressive headcount cut targets as a potential 600,000 personnel cut due to robotization. This might be an excessively aggressive forecast, but even cutting headcount by another 50,000 in 2026 will mean $7.5 billion in structural annual cost savings (if we assume that each employee on average costs the company $150k/year).

Reasons to avoid AMZN

AWS is still by far ahead of its main rivals in terms of market share, but I think that the cloud business is the one where competition risks are the highest. Microsoft (Azure) and Google are the closest rivals, both very strong competitors with their own cash cows that support aggressive investments in cloud and AI infrastructures. Google's recent release of its Gemini 3 large language model was extremely successful, which might create problems for Amazon over the long-term as cloud customers might switch to Google due to its strong LLM offering. All three players are investing loads of money in R&D, and there is no guarantee that Amazon will be able to win this fierce AI race. Losing the AI race might undermine the growth potential of AWS.

With its 4% YTD share price increase, Amazon is the worst-performing “Magnificent 7” stock in 2025. Main rivals in the AI race, Google and Microsoft, rallied by 64% and 15% over the same period, respectively. AMZN's underperformance compared to the main rivals might mean that I am overestimating the strength of its business mix.

Last but not least, despite the bullish forward P/E ratio setup, Amazon's P/S ratio is quite high compared to the company's historical averages. This means that extremely aggressive revenue growth expectations are priced in, and negative revenue revisions, even if EPS continues to grow, might be a notable share price headwind.

The verdict

Amazon is just too fundamentally strong to give up buying this stellar company. I expect the company to demonstrate a massive P/E ratio contraction over the next few years, which reinforces the Strong Buy case.

data.title: Harbor Disciplined Bond ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The traditional investing doctrines may not carry the same weight as they once did. The Fed shifted its focus to the labor market, despite inflation persistently above its 2% target. - Income Research + Management

Market in Review

In the third quarter of 2025, investors looked beyond the tariff-related impacts that plagued the second quarter, at least temporarily, and turned their attention to potential monetary policy changes, as well as threats to their independence. Economic data throughout the quarter painted a picture of softening labor markets and lingering inflation, despite relatively stable economic growth. U.S. job growth notably slowed, with nonfarm payrolls rising just 22,000 in August, far below forecasts, and downward adjustments were made for both June and July. The Bureau of Labor Statistics retroactively lowered the preliminary benchmark by 911,000 jobs for the prior 12 months – or roughly 76,000 fewer jobs per month. However, the unemployment rate edged up only slightly to 4.3%, a historically low level, suggesting we have not yet seen a pickup in layoffs. In September, the Federal Reserve (“Fed”) cut the fed funds target range by 0.25% to 4.00%–4.25%. This reduction, which was the first since December 2024, was considered a “risk-management cut.” Chair Powell acknowledged the cut was necessary as the labor market had weakened, despite persistent inflation above the 2% target. The August Consumer Price Index grew 2.9%, year over year, slightly above June and July’s 2.7% growth. Two additional cuts are expected in 2025, as evidenced by the updated dot plot. The Treasury curve steepened as a result with front-end rates falling the most in anticipation of further monetary policy easing. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased by 0.12%, to 3.61%, and the 10-year rate fell by 0.08%, to 4.15%.

Portfolio Performance

During the third quarter of 2025, the Harbor Disciplined Bond ETF (“ETF”) returned 2.23% (NAV), outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index, which returned 2.03%.

The ETF’s security selection within Financials, Industrials, and agency residential mortgage-backed securities (“RMBS”) contributed to returns during the quarter. From an asset allocation perspective, our underweight to Treasuries was a significant contributor to excess return during the period. Overweights to both the commercial mortgage-backed securities (“CMBS”) and Financial sectors were also additive.

The corporate market showed little sign of cooling. Healthy fundamentals and heavy issuance were met with insatiable demand, despite historically tight spread levels. Fundamentals were relatively stable, quarter over quarter, with leverage close to unchanged and earnings growing by 13%, although margins deteriorated slightly. Investment-grade and high-yield supply totaled over $387 billion and $118 billion, respectively. September, in particular, accounted for $207 billion and $58 billion, respectively – new records for the month of September. Despite heavy supply, spreads continued to tighten, with investment-grade spreads narrowing by 0.09% to 0.74%, and high-yield spreads declining by 0.23% to 2.67%. Lower-quality issuers outperformed higher-quality alternatives, with BBBs and CCCs leading their respective rating cohorts.

Securitized sectors outperformed Treasuries but could not keep pace with corporates. Agency mortgage-backed securities benefited from lower interest-rate volatility. Mortgage rates fell from 6.80% to 6.36% – a level not seen since early 2023 – but housing turnover remained low due to the lock-in effect of current homeowners. Asset-backed securities lagged other securitized sectors, given their shorter duration, but still outperformed Treasuries. Year-to-date supply of $277 billion was in line with last year’s pace. The prospect of lower rates and accommodative monetary policy supported non-agency CMBS. Issuance surpassed $114 billion, 41% higher than this time last year, with more than half coming from the single-asset single-borrower subsector.

Contributors and Detractors

The greatest contributors to performance included SBL Holdings, Aptiv, Ford Motor Credit, Brookfield Finance, and a Fannie Mae pool. The largest detractors from the ETF's performance included a Freddie Mac pool, Navient Corp, Charter Communications, Standard Building Solutions, and Oracle. We continue to be constructive on these holdings and will continue to hold these securities.

Positioning

We strive to remain duration neutral and curve neutral to the benchmark.

Positive contributors to relative performance included our security section within the agency RMBS, Financial, and Industrial sectors. Relative performance was also helped by our overweight to Financials, and CMBS, as well as our underweight to Treasuries. Detractors from relative performance included an out-of-index allocation to Small Business Administration ("SBAs").

The ETF's security selection within the AA, AAA, and BAA credit buckets, as well as our overweight to BAAs and underweight to Aas, aided relative performance. On the other hand, our underweight to the A credit bucket detracted from relative performance.

The ETF's out-of-benchmark allocations to the SBA and RMBS sectors detracted from relative returns.

We invest exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-income securities.

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we purchased Atlas Warehouse Lend in the secondary market at a spread of 1.50% versus a 3-year Treasury. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

We purchased FN CB3108 in the secondary market at a spread of 0.29% versus a 10-year Treasury. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

We also purchased FN MA4305 in the secondary market at a spread of 0.21% versus a 10-year Treasury. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

We sold Foundry JV Holdco 6.15% 2032 as an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

We sold FN FM2971 4.00% 2032 as an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

We also sold FN FS4922 3.00% 2034 as an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

At the start of the third quarter, the ETF was underweight Treasuries and overweight securitized. During the third quarter, we increased our exposure to Treasuries by 2.54%, decreasing the underweight to 24.27%. The ETF reduced its overweight to securitized to 19.79%, a decrease of 1.53%. The decrease in the securitized overweight was achieved by reducing our overweight to underweight within agency fixed-rate pass-throughs by 2.17%. Within credit, we trimmed our overweight to Financials by 1.59%, changed our underweight to overweight in Industrials by increasing the relative allocation by 1.15%, reduced our underweight to Utilities by 0.30%, while our underweight to non-corporates remained relatively flat at 3.24%.

Country Allocation

The ETF's country allocation relative to the benchmark did not change during the quarter. IR+M invests exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-income securities. With that said, consistent with our bottom-up approach, we will opportunistically purchase Yankee issues when we believe they are attractive on a relative value basis.

Over the quarter, the ETF's absolute weight in Yankees decreased materially to 9.75%.

Outlook

The traditional investing doctrines may not carry the same weight as they once did. The Fed shifted its focus to the labor market, despite inflation persistently above its 2% target. Changes to the composition of the Fed could also play a meaningful role in monetary policy for the foreseeable future. Meanwhile, the U.S.' fiscal trajectory is expected to deteriorate further as deficits and interest expense swell. This leads to a potentially very different economy than we have experienced in recent years. As bottom-up security selectors, we de-emphasize aggregated metrics while differentiating the leaders from laggards. We believe that ability, paired with our nimbleness, are qualities that should continue to help us navigate whichever way the market evolves.

High-yield investing poses additional credit risk related to lower-rated bonds.

Current 30-Day Yield-Unsub is the Current 30-Day Unsubsidized SEC Yield and does not reflect reimbursements or waivers of fees currently in effect.

Securities that receive no rating from an independent agency have been categorized as 'not rated.' Certain unrated securities (such as derivatives) are not reflected in the data shown. U.S. Treasury and U.S. Agency securities appear under the category U.S. Government/Agency. The credit quality of securities in the Fund's portfolio does not apply to the stability or safety of the Fund. The Fund itself has not been rated by an independent rating agency.

Attribution Disclosures

All data for this attribution analysis is provided by Income Research + Management.

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company.

Other is the contribution to relative return due to the cumulative differences in carry, rolldown, and convexity.

Shape is the contribution to relative return due to the difference between the portfolio's and the index's duration profiles. The basic formula is the [(difference of each key rate duration between the portfolio and the index) multiplied by the total return of the respective key rate Treasury] minus the Duration and Other effects.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Average Market Coupon: The average rate of the coupons of the bonds in a fund, weighted based each bond holding's size relative to the portfolio.

Duration is a commonly used measure of the sensitivity of the price of a debt security, or aggregate market value of a portfolio of debt securities, to change in interest rates. Modified Duration measures the change in the value of a security in response to the change in interest rates. Securities with a longer duration are more sensitive to changes in interest rates and generally have more volatile prices than securities of comparable quality with a shorter duration.

data.title: InfraCap MLP ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Returns in the midstream master limited partnership (MLP) sector underperformed the broader stock market in the third quarter of 2025. During the quarter, the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index (Benchmark Index) was down 2.01%, while the S&P 500® Index was up 8.11%. Additionally, energy commodities were mixed, with West Texas Intermediate crude prices down 4.21%, natural gas liquids (NGL) up 3.58%, and natural gas (12-month-average forward prices) down 5.78%.

During the quarter, natural gas distribution companies outperformed the broader midstream market, as price-sensitive companies felt the negative impact from commodity price declines. Over the last 18 months, natural gas distribution companies outperformed other midstream companies on the potential for growing power demand from data centers. Notably, xAI announced a $20 billion dollar capital raise for its Colossus 2 data center.

We believe that greater demand will increase the value of storage and pipeline distribution networks. Williams Cos. (WMB), Kinder Morgan (KMI), TC Energy Corp. (TRP), and DT Midstream (DTM) could be well positioned to benefit from this trend. While the growth potential from data center power demand is still in an early stage, these companies have indicated ongoing conversations with counterparties that we believe will benefit them once formalized and announced in 2026.

The U.S. Federal Reserve acknowledged meaningful progress toward lowering inflation in 3Q and indicated that the balance of risks has shifted to employment. We expect upcoming data releases to confirm that inflation has moderated. Importantly, midstream companies may benefit from pipeline contracts linked to inflation, so they are naturally hedged against a higher inflation scenario. Therefore, midstream could perform well in both a soft-landing environment with low inflation and a longer-lasting higher-inflation environment.

How the Fund Performed

During the quarter, the Fund had a net return of -4.85%. This compares to a net return of -2.01% for the Benchmark Index. Relative to the Benchmark Index, the Fund's leverage and interest expenses detracted 0.84%, security selection and call writing added 0.56%, while fees and taxes detracted 2.56%. The Fund was negatively impacted by a 2.32% tax expense.

The Fund paid a monthly dividend of $0.29 per share for the last three distributions. Net asset value per share at quarter-end was $40.28 and the Fund's 30-day SEC yield 1 was 8.29%.

Top Contributors

  • Western Midstream (WES) announced an agreement to acquire Aris Water Solutions (ARIS) for $1.5 billion.
  • Enterprise Products (EPD) reported a slight earnings miss in the second quarter.
  • Delek Logistics (DKL) continues to issue small amounts of equity and grow in the Benchmark Index.
  • Phillips 66 (PSX) benefited from a 7.5% increase in forward crack spreads (refining margins) during the quarter.
  • Venture Global (VG) reported second-quarter earnings that were 5% above expectations.

Bottom Contributors

  • Hess Midstream (HESM) announced that it expects to come in at the low end of guidance.
  • Sunoco LP (SUN) reported earnings that were 2.4% below expectations.
  • Plains All American (PAA) was impacted by weakness in NGL-basis differentials.
  • Energy Transfer (ET) continues to be well positioned to benefit from data center power demand.
  • Mplx Lp (MPLX) continues to trade with the lowest volatility in the group.

Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, we utilized proceeds to reallocate toward higher-conviction names and took advantage of positive relative performance, while maintaining appropriate diversification.

Add: Williams Cos. (WMB) Inc. (WMB) is benefiting from data centers (xAI, Fermi America).

Add: Kinder Morgan Inc (KMI) is benefiting from data centers (xAI, Fermi America).

Reduce: Western Midstream Partners LP (WES) was impacted by lower rig count and completions on its acreage.

Reduce: Sunoco LP (SUN) missed earnings and does not benefit from natural gas data center demand.

Outlook

We believe that MLPs, which were once widely recognized as a go-to vehicle for income-focused investors, can be a useful tool for investors looking for high income in a falling-interest-rate environment. Over the past few years, midstream companies successfully transitioned to positive free cash flow, sustainable dividends, and reasonable leverage levels. Earnings, which were historically low in the MLP space, are now substantially positive, and companies trade at reasonable price-to-earnings ratios of less than 12x. Additionally, debt-to-EBITDA, a measure of leverage, has fallen from an average of 4.5x to 3.6x. These developments support modest distribution growth, while facilitating the financing of capital expenditures from free cash flows. In 2025/2026, we expect midstream companies to continue transitioning to share buybacks and modest distribution growth.

We continue to monitor geopolitical tensions, OPEC+ (Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Companies) induced supply changes, and disciplined domestic exploration and production (E&P) budgets. Saudi Arabia increased oil production ahead of expectations earlier this year to counter noncompliance by other OPEC+ member countries. Due to the additional production from Saudi Arabia, and the impacts of tariffs on demand, we are now forecasting $60–$80 per barrel for crude oil in 2025/2026. As an offset, the U.S. rig count has fallen by more than 7% year-to-date, which will reduce supply. Still, we continue to view midstream favorably, as E&P companies remain disciplined and midstream benefits from NGL export opportunities.

data.title: Gabelli Enterprise Mergers & Acquisitions Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Elevated M&A volume activity continued in the third quarter with $1.3 trillion in announced transactions. This follows $1.1 trillion in the first quarter and $1.06 trillion in the second quarter. The total volume of $3.4 trillion in the first 9 months represents a 32% increase relative to the same period in 2024. However, the number of deals dropped to 8,912, the worst third quarter in 20 years.

The quarter was led by megadeals. One of the largest deals in recent years was Union Pacific (UNP)'s $85 billion acquisition of Norfolk Southern. The largest going private transaction in history was announced during the quarter: the $55 billion acquisition of Electronic Arts (EA) by Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, Silver Lake, and Affinity Partners. Other notable deals were Anglo American's $17 billion merger of equals with Teck and Palo Alto Networks' $25 billion acquisition of CyberArk.

Technology and financials continued to be the top sectors for M&A. These two sectors accounted for over $1 trillion in deal activity during the first nine months of 2025. Private equity-backed transactions as well as cross-border transactions kept up with their recent pace.

The regulatory environment has continued to be viewed favorably by market participants. The antitrust agencies have shown a willingness to accept remedies and divestitures when appropriate, enabling more transactions to proceed. This evolving approach has begun to restore corporate confidence in the regulatory process, and is expected to support continued strength in new deal activity.

Several macro factors, including geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainty, remain unresolved. However, the expectation is that the several months of a pickup in M&A activity since the spring will continue, driven by strategic imperatives such as the need to compete globally in the face of shifting tariff dynamics, access to new technologies, and entry into emerging growth verticals.

Deals in the Pipeline

Metsera, Inc. (MTSR) (0.1% of net assets as of September 30, 2025) (MTSR - $52.33 - NASDAQ) is a biopharmaceutical company accelerating the next generation of medicines for obesity and metabolic diseases. MTSR agreed to be acquired for $47.50 per share by Pfizer on September 22. MTSR shareholders will also receive a CVR with a value up to $22.50 per share. The transaction remains subject to shareholder approval and U.S. antitrust approval. It is expected to be completed as early as 4Q 2025.

Tourmaline Bio, Inc. (TRML) (1.3%) (TRML - $47.83 - NASDAQ) is a biopharmaceutical company focused on developing pacibekitug, an anti-IL-6 mAb, as a treatment option for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. TRML agreed to be acquired for $48.00 per share in cash by Roche on September 9. The deal remains subject to U.S. antitrust approval. The tender offer will expire in late October and is expected to close soon thereafter.

Verint Systems Inc. (VRNT) (1.9%) (VRNT - $20.25 - NASDAQ) is a provider of call center and automation software. VRNT agreed to be acquired for $20.50 per share in cash by Thoma Bravo on August 25. The transaction requires shareholder approval and various antitrust approvals, including the U.S. The transaction is expected to close in late 2025 or January 2026.

Done Deals

Dun & Bradstreet Holdings, Inc. provides data and analytics solutions to financial services firms. After months of reports of a potential sale, on March 24, 2025, the company agreed to be acquired by Clearlake for $9.15 per share in cash. The deal was completed on August 26, 2025.

Enstar Group Limited is a global property & casualty company with a focus on run-off insurance policies. On July 29, 2024, the company agreed to be acquired by Sixth Street for $338.00 per share in cash. While it took almost an entire year to obtain all the necessary insurance approvals in the U.S. and internationally, the deal was finally completed on July 2, 2025.

SpringWorks Therapeutics, Inc. is a biotech company focused on treatment of various forms of cancer and other rare diseases, particularly ovarian and endometrial tumors and cancers. On April 28, 2025, the company agreed to be acquired by Germany-based Merck KGaA for $47.00 per share in cash. After receiving US and German antitrust approvals followed by shareholder approval, the deal closed on July 1, 2025.

data.title: Lithium Junior Miners News For The Month Of December 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the December 2025 edition of the 'junior' lithium miner news. We have categorized those lithium miners that are not in commercial production as the juniors.

Note: Investors are reminded that many of the lithium juniors will most likely not be needed until the late 2020's (& 2030's) to supply the potentially booming electric vehicle [EV] and battery energy stationary storage [BESS] markets. This means investing in these companies requires a higher risk tolerance and a longer time frame.

The past month China lithium carbonate spot prices and spodumene prices were significantly higher.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported lithium spodumene concentrate Index (Li2O 5.5%-6.2%, excluding tax/insurance/freight) spot price of USD 1,378, as of December 22, 2025, significantly higher the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price was significantly higher last month, now at CNY 99,500. The CIF Asia lithium carbonate price is ~US$11.38/kg.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 99,500 (~USD 14,156) (source)

Rio Tinto forecasts lithium emerging supply gap (chart from 2021) - 60 new mines the size of Jadar needed

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid 2025) (source)

Lithium market news

For a summary of the latest lithium market news and the 'major' lithium company's news, investors can read: "Lithium Miners News For The Month Of December 2025" article. Highlights include:

  • Shipments of #BESS cells are surging in 2025, set to exceed 550 GWh this year, a 76% y-o-y increase.
  • U.S. Export-Import Bank plans to spend $100B for critical minerals, energy dominance...Nuclear energy will be a focus.
  • Tianqi Lithium expects global lithium demand to reach 2 million tonnes of LCE by 2026, essentially achieving a supply-demand balance.
  • Battery pack prices fall in 2025 down 8% to $108/kWh, despite rising metal prices. The lowest observed cell and pack prices were just $36/kWh and $50/kWh used for LFP batteries going into stationary storage applications.
  • Lithium bulls are betting on energy storage systems as the next meaningful pillar of demand for the battery metal, nudging the global market back toward balance after years of oversupply.
  • EU launches €3 billion plan to boost critical minerals sector. Stockpiling and public-private partnerships are central to the EU’s strategy.
  • Ford scraps $6.5 billion EV battery contract with Korea’s LG.
  • JP Morgan predicts lithium prices will bounce back 62% as large-scale energy storage projects ramp up. It lifted its 2026 and 2027 price forecasts for lithium to $17,500/t and $22,000/t.
  • Lithium markets hit fever pitch as China revokes more permits.

LibertyStream Infrastructure Partners Inc. [TSXV:LIB] - formerly Volt Lithium [TSXV:VLT] (VLTLF)

LibertyStream produces first lithium carbonate from automated refining unit in Texas Permian Basin, demonstrating end-to-end field-scale production.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • H2 2025 - Start of small scale commercial production from Permian Basin oilfield brine.

  • "Nano-filtration plant has been assembled and tested off-site and is now in transit to Hombre Muerto West (HMW).
  • Phase 1 construction activities at HMW continue to advance toward completion, ahead of expected first production of lithium chloride in H1 2026."

data.title: Lots Of Things Are Looking Up
data.description:
Valued readers, please excuse me. For the past few months, I've suffered from writer's block complicated by a lack of government-produced data. I now have some facts to work with, and they look pretty good. I've assembled a baker's dozen of my favorite charts here, and I will try to keep the commentary lean and let the charts do the heavy lifting. It feels a lot like Christmas!

To sum up: The economy is in decent shape (2-3% growth) and inflation remains subdued (2.5% or less). More specifically, the M2 money supply is growing at a very moderate 4.5% rate, and most, if not all, of the $6 trillion increase in M2 that was "printed" during the Covid era has been absorbed. There is still no evidence that Trump's tariffs have boosted inflation. The main source of slightly-above-target inflation in recent years can be traced to the government's faulty measurement of housing and shelter costs, and these have finally subsided and should remain low (if not negative) for the foreseeable future.

Commodity prices (abstracting from gold, which appears to inhabit an alternative universe) are very well-behaved and haven't shown any meaningful increase for years. GDP growth was surprisingly strong in Q3 '25, and Trump's Big Beautiful tax cuts, coupled with impressive deregulation and an actual shrinkage of the public sector workforce, have set the stage for continuing growth in the coming year. It is comforting to see a 4-5% increase in business investment so far this year (e.g., capital goods orders and shipments), and it is now common knowledge that AI is already contributing to improved productivity. Finally, it is also very comforting to see that federal government spending has not increased at all over the past 12 months, while revenues have surged by almost 10%!

Chart #1

Chart #1 shows the level of the M2 money supply, arguably the best measure of readily spendable money. It grew at a 6% pace from 1995 through 2007, during which time inflation averaged about 2%. Then, all hell broke lose: M2 surged by some $6 trillion in a 2-year period, fueled by Covid stimulus spending which was effectively monetized. That bulge now has all but disappeared.

Chart #2

Chart #2 shows the 6-mo. annualized growth rate of M2, now a mere 4.6%. The economy is now on a low-inflation monetary diet. As I have said about M2 many times in the past 5 years, this is arguably the biggest news that no one, not even the Fed, is paying attention to.

Chart #3

Chart #3 shows the ratio of M2 to nominal GDP. I call this the "demand for money," since it essentially measures how much of our annual incomes we prefer to hold in the form of money. Money demand surged in the first year or so of Covid, because people were terrified and largely unable or unwilling to spend all the cash the government was doling out. Strong money demand effectively neutralized the surge in the M2 money supply, which explains why inflation didn't start rising for a year after M2 began to soar. Money demand then collapsed beginning in mid-2022 as things began to return to normal, and that effectively offset the collapse and actual shrinkage of M2 - that's why a contracting money supply didn't cause the recession that was so widely anticipated. (Remember: inflation happens only when the supply of money exceeds the demand to hold it.) Now money demand is almost all the way back to where it was prior to Covid.

Chart #4

Chart #5

Chart #4 compares the strength or weakness of the dollar to the level of inflation-adjusted spot commodity prices, while Chart #5 shows the nominal level of those same commodities since early 2020. These are all very basic commodities, not the sort that are subject to speculative pressures (like gold can be). What we see here is a very strong inverse correlation between the dollar and real commodity prices. A stronger dollar tends to coincide with weaker commodity prices, and vice versa. Note that commodity prices over the past 33 years haven't changed at all in real terms, and the dollar has only strengthened modestly. Over the past several years, commodity prices have gone nowhere - a strong symptom of an absence of underlying inflationary pressures.

Chart #6

Chart #6 is structured the same way as Chart #4, except that I've used the inflation-adjusted price of crude oil. In real terms, oil prices are relatively low, and that reinforces the outlook for low and stable inflation. Oil is the most volatile of all commodity prices, and the price of energy is an important contributor to the economy's health. Nationwide gasoline prices are currently just under $3 per gallon, and that is roughly what gasoline prices have averaged over the past 20 years. This is very good news for economic growth.

Chart #7

Chart #7 looks at the short-term, annualized rate of change in Owners' Equivalent Rent, which in turn constitutes about one-third of the CPI. In the past two months, this important component of the CPI has decelerated markedly; this will subtract meaningfully from reported inflation over the next 10 months.

Chart #8

Chart #8 compares the year-over-year change in the total CPI to its ex-shelter version. The gap between the red and blue lines over the past 2 years is largely due to the government over-estimating shelter costs. The gap has now all but closed.

Chart #9

Chart #9 shows the long-term growth trend of real GDP (green line), which averaged about 3.1% per year from the post-war period through 2007. Sadly, the economy has yet to return to those glory days; growth has averaged only a bit more than 2.3% per year since the Great Recession ended in 2009. Why this huge shortfall? I suspect a variety of culprits: massive increases in transfer payments (e.g., green energy subsidies, Obamacare), burdensome regulations (e.g., CAFE standards), and DEI hiring, to name a few. Stepping back, mankind has spent several trillions of dollars on inefficient energy projects in a vain attempt to "save" the planet from climate change. A return to efficient energy investment appears already to be underway. This is great news for the planet and its economies.

Chart #10

Chart #10 tracks the growth of private sector jobs in recent years, which are now growing at a snail's pace. Contrast this to the surprisingly strong growth of Q3 '25 GDP (4.3%) and you must conclude that productivity is on the rise by more than enough to offset the drag of massive deportations of illegals. Meanwhile, federal government payrolls have shrunk by 9% (273K) so far this year! To my mind, that's the equivalent of pouring much less sand into the wheels of commerce.

Chart #11

Chart #11 shows the inflation-adjusted price of gold over the past 113 years. Monetarists like me have trouble reconciling soaring gold prices with an apparent absence of inflation pressures. Central banks have meaningfully increased their purchases of gold in the past four years, and that at least partially explains gold's rise to levels never before seen - or even imagined. On the other hand, this could be a classic case of speculative froth which eventually exhausts itself and collapses. The 30% collapse in Bitcoin prices since early October could be a harbinger of trouble ahead for other markets.

Chart #12

Chart #12 paints a disturbing picture, suggesting that the recent collapse in bitcoin could be presaging a similar decline in equity prices. The market cap of cryptocurrencies peaked at $4.28 trillion on October 6th, and currently stands at $2.95 trillion; $1.33 trillion of paper wealth has thus evaporated in a matter of weeks. Dabble in gold and bitcoin at your peril. I wouldn't touch the stuff - give me real, productive assets instead.

Chart #13

Chart #13, in contrast, paints a hopeful picture; federal government finances look to be returning to some measure of sanity. Federal government spending has been flat for the past year, while revenues have increased by 10%. The days of $2 trillion annual deficits are fading fast. Federal debt owed to the public has been 90-100% of GDP for over 5 years (it peaked at 103% in mid-2020) and may soon begin to decline. In the meantime, the true burden of our national debt is currently 3.7% of GDP, and that is significantly less than the 4.5-5% levels which prevailed during the 1980s. There is still reason to be optimistic.

Happy New Year!

Original Post

data.title: Templeton Foreign Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equity markets collectively advanced through the third quarter of 2025 as tariff anxieties gradually eased, central banks adopted a more accommodative policy stance, and leadership among technology-related stocks persisted.
  • Detractors: The fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index-NR, for the quarter, primarily due to stock selection in the industrials, utilities and materials sectors.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the financials, consumer discretionary and communication services sectors contributed to relative performance for the quarter, as did an overweight in the consumer discretionary sector.
  • Outlook: In broad terms, global equity markets have continued to shrug off geopolitical risks and macroeconomic uncertainty, driving many equity indexes to new highs. We see no immediate reason for this positive trend to reverse, though we remain vigilant in monitoring global developments.

Performance Review

  • Global equity markets collectively advanced through the third quarter of 2025 as tariff anxieties gradually eased, central banks adopted a more accommodative policy stance, and leadership among technology-related stocks persisted. July's market tone improved amid clearer trade signals and generally resilient US corporate earnings; August saw equity gains despite episodic volatility around new tariff measures; and September capped the quarter with a US Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cut that steadied rate-sensitive assets and lifted risk appetite. The Fed lowered its target rate by 25 basis points, while the European Central Bank and the Bank of England held their policy rates steady; the Bank of Japan kept its key rate unchanged but continued its stance of edging toward policy normalization. Together, these dynamics, along with calmer rate volatility and encouraging corporate results, underpinned risk assets into quarter-end.
  • The fund (Advisor class without sales charges) underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World ex-US Index-NR, for the quarter, primarily due to stock selection in the industrials, utilities and materials sectors.
  • Global agricultural equipment producer CNH Industrial was a top detractor for the quarter, as its shares were impacted by weak guidance from peers Deere (not held by the fund) and Caterpillar (not held by the fund) amid renewed US tariff pressures. Despite near-term softness, we believe CNH remains a mispriced cyclical company with meaningful self-help potential. In our view, management's focus on pricing discipline, manufacturing quality, and dealer network upgrades—alongside integration of precision agriculture technology—should help drive structurally higher profit margins and improved through-cycle cash returns. Although recent results underscore short-term headwinds, we view these risks as largely priced in and continue to hold a constructive long-term outlook.
  • Stock selection in the financials, consumer discretionary and communication services sectors contributed to relative performance for the quarter, as did an overweight in the consumer discretionary sector.
  • China-based Alibaba Group Holding, a multinational company specializing in e-commerce, retail, internet and technology, was the top individual stock contributor. We view the company as a proxy for China's consumption activity. We believe Alibaba should benefit from improved management execution in regrowing both its domestic e-commerce and cloud businesses. Additionally, management has committed itself to significantly growing shareholder returns over fiscal years 2025-2027. Its shares rose during the third quarter due to strong performance in its cloud computing and e-commerce segments, driven by growth in AI-related products and services.
  • Geographically, stock selection in Asia ex Japan was the most significant contributor to relative performance for the quarter. Stock selection and an overweight in non-eurozone/non-UK Europe detracted from relative results.

Outlook

  • In broad terms, global equity markets have continued to shrug off geopolitical risks and macroeconomic uncertainty, driving many equity indexes to new highs. We see no immediate reason for this positive trend to reverse, though we remain vigilant in monitoring global developments. The key question for US and global equities is whether the market broadens beyond the current AI-focused leadership. Our view is that it is possible, and the fund should be reasonably well positioned for such a rotation. We remain selective in the IT sector, with financials and health care among our preferred sectors.
  • In the Asia Pacific (APAC) region, we similarly maintain a bottom-up selective approach even as our outlook on APAC potentially improves. While not yet expensive, we believe regional valuations have increased and risk/reward adjustments remain key. Potential venues where we see mispricing opportunities include underappreciated non-AI technology companies in Taiwan, along with South Korean stocks.
  • In Europe, we believe the combination of policy support, structural investment and attractive valuations underpin a constructive outlook. Market breadth is another key feature, in our view, offering a more diversified potential opportunity set across industries and sectors such as luxury, health care, industrials and electrification technologies.

data.title: Commercial Metals Shifting To A Higher-Margin, Less-Volatile Business Mix
data.description:
By and large, when a company is good at something, investors are right to be wary when that company ventures away from its core and into new businesses. In the case of Commercial Metals Company (CMC), though, the company’s decision to further diversify into complementary construction materials like precast concrete makes a lot of sense from a margin and capital intensity perspective, as well as from a growth perspective.

Commercial Metals has done pretty well since my last update despite a weaker infrastructure market than I’d expected. Shares are up close to 45% since that last article, slightly outperforming Steel Dynamics (STLD) and outperforming rebar rival Nucor (NUE) by a wider margin.

I like the improved revenue, margin, and cash flow outlook that Commercial Metals’ recent acquisitions bring to the company. Valuation is more challenging, though, and while I’m bullish on rebar pricing and Commercial Metals’ likelihood of executing well in the coming years, it’s tougher to make the numbers work now.

Import Restrictions Are Canceling Out New Capacity, And Demand Seems To Be Improving

There have been some valid concerns in the rebar market that new capacity equivalent to around 15% of industry capacity in the U.S. would lead to weaker prices, as demand has been softer than expected throughout much of 2025 due to weaker non-residential activity and disappointing infrastructure activity.

Offsetting this has been good news on the protectionism front. Between Section 232 tariffs and recent anti-dumping investigations against major exporters to the U.S. (including a recent decision against Algeria that is likely to be a pattern for future findings against Bulgaria, Egypt, and Vietnam), those recent domestic capacity additions are likely to be more or less offset by reduced imports, as tariffs and anti-dumping penalties will effectively make that steel uneconomical.

Between those tariffs and some signs of vigor on the demand side, pricing has been improving. Rebar prices were soft for the first half of the year—trading around $750 to $775 on average in Q1 and Q2 (after around $775 in FY’24 and $908 in FY’23)—but picked up to over $850 in the third quarter of 2025 and recently moved over $900/t as per Platts on December 19, 2025.

Looking at 2026, I see a mixed outlook. Infrastructure spending in the wake of the passage of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (or IIJA) has been disappointing thus far, with states and municipalities slower to move on projects and inflation eroding the value of the original sums offered in the bill. With that, about 60% of the IIJA funds remain to be spent, though there are some risks tied to reauthorization later in 2026. Projects are moving through the pipeline, though, and I do expect a low-to-mid single-digit improvement in activity in 2026.

Non-residential construction is likewise “mixed” at best today. This has been a weak year, with Dodge showing non-resi starts by square footage down 11% in November and down 7% year-to-date, with weakness in areas like office, retail, and warehouse offsetting strength in areas like data centers and manufacturing plants (reshoring). While the Dodge Momentum Index has been strong for much of the year, the Architectural Billings Index (a more near-term-oriented index) has contracted in 35 of the last 38 months and remains below 50.

I’m expecting a pickup in 2026. It’s debatable whether the Fed’s rate cuts to date will be enough to revive non-resi starts in the first half of 2026, but I do believe those stronger Dodge readings will translate into more activity in H2’26, and I expect further strength from data centers and manufacturing construction.

All of this should be supportive of healthy rebar prices in 2026. I don’t expect the recent $900+ prices to hold, particularly in the first half of the year, but I think $875+ for the year is possible, and I expect that strength to continue into 2027 as non-resi continues to recover and infrastructure projects continue toward completion (as anybody who has dealt with roadworks can attest, it takes time to finish these projects).

This, in turn, should be supportive of stronger shipments and realized prices for CMC in 2026. Moreover, with input costs seemingly under control, steel margins and EBITDA per ton should both be significantly healthier in 2026 and 2027.

Diversification Looks Like A Good Move

CMC management had been signaling for some time that they were on the hunt for acquisitions, and particularly that they were looking to further build up complementary construction offerings in the Emerging Business segment (in other words, they weren’t looking to acquire more rebar or long steel capacity).

CMC then announced two deals in relatively quick succession—the $675M acquisition of Concrete Pipe & Precast (for about 9.5x EBITDA, pre-tax) and then the $1.84B acquisition of Foley Products (for about 10.3x EBITDA, pre-tax).

I like these deals. Not only do they fit as complementary assets to support infrastructure and non-residential construction markets, but I also like the businesses themselves. Precast concrete essentially involves pouring concrete into reusable forms/molds at company plants and then delivering the cured pieces to the construction site—saving time and labor at the site. This fits directly with contractors looking to maintain smoother timelines (and cut as much of the “hurry up and wait” processes as possible) and do more with less labor.

It also happens to be a profitable business. CPP’s EBITDA margins are in the low-to-mid-20s, well ahead of even CMC’s best years, and Foley’s margins are in the 40s, and both are considerably less capital-intensive than steel, and their input prices are generally less volatile.

I believe precast/preformed concrete is a growth opportunity. It represents less than 20% of the U.S. concrete market today, and it’s a fragmented business (the top 10 account for less than 25% of the market); CMC will be the 3rd-largest player, the largest in the Southeast, and one of the few to operate on a nearly national scale. As I believe preformed products can easily grow to a third of the market, this is a business that I believe can support future organic reinvestment and/or future tuck-in deals to add desired regional coverage.

The Outlook

Between a recovery in the core rebar market and non-residential end-market (and further improvement in infrastructure demand), I believe CMC can generate close to 6% annualized revenue growth over the next five years and long-term growth around 5%.

At this point my FY’26 and FY’27 revenue estimates are above the sell-side averages (by about 5%), but not quite at the high end of the sell-side range. While my EBITDA estimates are well above the sell-side averages (14.75% and 16.75% adjusted margins), I believe the averages don’t yet capture all of the post-CPP/Foley estimate changes.

In any case, over time I believe this diversification and improved profitability in rebar (through restricting imports) will add around 350bp to CMC’s full-cycle adjusted EBITDA margins and help drive around 250bp of long-term incremental free cash flow margins. That should, in turn, drive double-digit annualized FCF growth, and I believe CMC will be able to pay down its net debt below 2x EBITDA relatively quickly (within a year), while also eventually rebuilding the dry powder for further M&A or organic expansion.

Neither discounted cash flow nor multiple-based approaches suggests significant undervaluation today. Discounted cash flow suggests around 7% long-term annualized total return potential, while an EV/EBITDA multiple of 7x (higher than historical averages to account for the meaningful boost in profitability and, I believe, consistency) and 1.5x forward book get me to around $70/share.

The Bottom Line

I don’t feel as though my near-term (two-year) assumptions are all that conservative, but I do see some potential for beat-and-raise quarters here, particularly if infrastructure and non-residential improve more than I expect. That’s an important possibility now, as I just can’t get to an attractive fair value otherwise.

Given the momentum in the business and the possibility of underestimating the synergies and upside from the deals, I can understand staying bullish here, but I struggle to find more than 10%-15% upside in the numbers now, and I can’t call that a “buy” without resorting to “just ignore the valuation and buy the story/momentum.”

data.title: Janus Henderson Mid Cap Value Managed Account Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 3.60% (gross) and the Russell Midcap® Value Index returned 6.18%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in financials and industrials detracted from relative performance, while stock selection in consumer staples and healthcare contributed.

Outlook

Even as we see potential sources of market volatility, we remain confident in our disciplined value investment approach.

Investment environment

  • Equity markets rose over the quarter, on positive corporate earnings, increased expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts, and artificial intelligence (AI) enthusiasm. The Fed lowered its target rate by 25 basis points in September, with the market expecting more rate cuts by year-end.
  • A “risk-on,” lower-quality market rally during the quarter resulted in numerous all-time highs achieved. This rally was led by high-beta, lower return-on-equity, and money-losing stocks.

Portfolio review

While our focus on high-quality value companies hindered relative performance in a high-volatility quarter, we remain committed to owning high-quality companies at reasonable valuations, a discipline we believe is especially important given current market valuations and the narrowness of the market performance.

Kirby, a transporter of petroleum and petrochemical products, hindered relative performance. While short-term demand and utilization have been weak for Kirby, we believe we will see pricing rebound along with demand over the long term, given a lack of industry investment in new capacity.

We had two notable detractors in the consumer discretionary sector. LKQ, which sells after-market automotive parts, weighed on relative returns due to soft demand from collision repair customers. It is likely that some lower-end consumers are choosing to pocket insurance proceeds rather than pay to repair their damaged autos, and this has been a headwind for LKQ. Despite weakness in the stock price, we are not adding to our position until we gain clarity regarding both cyclical and secular headwinds facing LKQ. Shares of retailer Bath and Body Works declined during the period after the company's new management team issued cautious guidance while indicating that it may take longer than previously expected to achieve sustained revenue growth. While the company continues to innovate with new products and themes, concerns about the lower price points for its products and the health of the lower-end consumer also pressured the stock. We remain invested as we believe the stock is attractively valued relative to fundamentals.

Teradyne, a supplier of testing equipment for semiconductors, contributed to relative performance, benefiting from rising capital spending on AI and other advanced computing. We remain invested in the stock but have trimmed some on recent strength.

Defense stocks such as L3Harris were strong relative performers, supported by accelerating global spending on defense and military equipment. We have maintained a sizeable position in L3Harris but have opportunistically trimmed our weighting given the less attractive reward/risk. BWX Technologies, another defense-exposed holding, also was a top performer on a relative basis. As the sole supplier of nuclear power systems to the U.S. Navy, the company is also pursuing other revenue growth opportunities through technologies that serve data-center power needs and the medical isotope industry. That said, we trimmed our position given the strong share price performance and expanded valuation.

The portfolio ended the quarter overweight in the industrials, materials, financials, and consumer discretionary sectors, while it was underweight in the real estate, utilities, consumer staples, energy, communication services, information technology, and healthcare sectors.

Manager outlook

Our sense is that many of the economic crosscurrents that the market has dealt with recently will likely continue for the foreseeable future. The lower-end consumer is strained and is unlikely to experience meaningful relief in the short term, given above-average inflation and a softening labor market. Offsetting this, the middle- to higher-end consumer is on relatively solid footing due to the wealth effect from hard assets and the appreciation of the stock market. Beyond the consumer, continued softness in transportation, usually a sign of a troubled economy, is being offset by the boom in AI- and data center-related spending. Additionally, there is

increased clarity regarding tariffs, the potential for the Fed to continue cutting rates, and tax-related stimulus that may kick in as we progress toward the end of the year. Thus, we may continue to see gains despite signs of froth in the market.

While volatility has been negligible thus far, it may increase given recent market imbalances. Excitement around AI has been a strong driver of market returns, and we have benefited from some of this excitement. But this has driven some stock valuations to elevated levels that may be unsupported by fundamentals. The focus on AI has also led investors to overlook compelling ideas in other parts of the market, providing a potentially good opportunity for stock selection, especially in the small- and mid-capitalization ranges. As always, we remain committed to our focus on quality as we seek out well-managed, profitable companies with low debt levels that we believe are undervalued by the market. We believe such companies have potential to outperform regardless of the economic backdrop.

data.title: Four Corners Property: Better Valuation, But Limited Near‑Term Upside
data.description:
Introduction

In February, I wrote my first article about Four Corners Property (FCPT). At that time, the share price was around $28; now, 10 months later, the share price has fallen to around $23.50.

At that point in time, I had a relatively bearish outlook toward the stock and rated it as a “hold.” I gave three main reasons for this rating:

  • Portfolio concentration: Almost 80% of ABR was coming from restaurants, which I expected to struggle due to consumer weakness.
  • Investment spread: I believed that the cap rate of their acquisitions was far too low and below their cost of capital.
  • Valuation: The two models I used yielded values of $23.86 and $25.25, respectively.

As the share price has dropped around 16% since then, I want to take another look to see whether the stock is now worth investing in.

Portfolio

While not a lot can happen in less than one year in terms of portfolio concentration, it is worth mentioning that management is taking small steps to address this issue. Every quarter they are reducing their dependence on their main tenant, Darden Restaurants, and increasing their exposure to different tenants and sectors, such as medical retail and auto services.

This will probably remain an issue for the foreseeable future, one that is not easy or quick to fix, but at this rate the exposure to Darden is likely to fall below 40% in the next five years.

Investment spread

The second bearish point is the spread between their property acquisition capitalization rate and their cost of capital. As of 30 September 2025, the average cap rate YTD was 6.8%, which is a bit lower than the 2024 cap rate of 7.1%.

While this may look like an ok-ish cap rate at first glance, it has to be compared to their cost of capital, since they are issuing equity and debt to acquire new properties.

Using the Kroll recommendation for the US equity risk premium as of September 2025 of 5.5% and the risk-free rate of 3.5% for the spot 20-year US Treasury yield for the same date, the cost of equity for the average US company is 9%, which should be a good approximation of Four Corners’ cost of equity.

Since Four Corners has a BBB credit rating from Fitch, we can look at the ICE BofA BBB US Corporate Index to get a good approximation of their cost of debt, which stands at approximately 5% right now.

Using the current capital structure of $2.49B (66.9%) of equity with a cost of equity of 9% and $1.23B (33.1%) of debt with a cost of debt of 5%, the resulting cost of capital is around 7.68%. As such, any acquisition with a cap rate below this number will destroy shareholder value.

The result? They are acquiring hundreds of properties every year without much to show for it. Growing the number of properties at 10% while AFFO per share grows at a meagre 2.0% to 2.5% per year is essentially negative real growth (considering inflation is around 3%). The smart thing to do here would be to stop trying to grow just for the sake of growth and instead focus on recycling capital until the macro environment improves.

Valuation

To determine the intrinsic value of the company and to keep things consistent, I will use the same two models as in the last article: the Gordon Growth Model and the Adjusted Funds from Operations Model.

Gordon Growth Model

Given the high current payout ratio of the company, around 86.5%, I expect the dividend growth to be a bit lower than the AFFO per share growth for the foreseeable future. As such, while I expect AFFO per share to grow at around 3% long term, I assume that the dividends per share will grow at only 2.5%.

The cost of equity was calculated using the methodology mentioned in the previous section, which is 9%.

Based on these assumptions, the intrinsic value implied by the model is $23.18, which means that the current market price of $23.44 is considered fair value to slightly overvalued.

Adjusted Funds from Operations Model

The Adjusted Funds from Operations Model is slightly more complex than the previous model. Instead of focusing on dividends, this approach places emphasis on cash flows generated by the business, how much cash the company will return in dividends, as well as a terminal value the company is likely to trade at by the end of the forecast period.

For the assumptions, I've estimated an AFFO per share growth rate of 3% in 2027 and 3.5% in 2028, 2029, and 2030. I expect the dividend payout ratio to contract to a level similar to peers (from 86.5% currently to around 80% in 2030). As such, I expect the dividend per share to grow more slowly than the AFFO per share. For the terminal value, I've used the current median multiple of peers of 14 times forward AFFO.

Based on the above assumptions, the model suggests a fair value of $23.25 per share, meaning that the current market price of $23.44 is fair and that the stock is expected to return close to its cost of equity (9%) going forward.

Conclusion

While the property portfolio has improved a bit and the valuation is certainly much better than the last time I analyzed the stock, I still see limited upside in the near future, as growth is unlikely to accelerate and multiples are unlikely to expand given the macroeconomic conditions. Therefore, I reaffirm the “hold” rating.
